hub_subtitle: Business English para seguros, reuniões e entrevistas — e o desbloqueio da fala, do primeiro dia ao último

lesson.menu_title: What Do You Like? What Do You Want? -- Preferences, Desires and Goals
lesson.menu_desc: A aula em que ela diz o que quer — e descobre que isso não é arrogância, é a resposta à pergunta. Marco de fechamento do bloco: a conversa de carreira da Zurich tem trinta minutos e uma pergunta só — "What do you want?" — e a Paula passou a vida inteira respondendo "tá tudo bem, obrigada". Três verbos que ela já quase tem: like + verbo com -ing (o que ela já faz e gosta), want to (direto) e would like to — que é só o want de gravata, o mesmo truque do can/could da aula 9. Seis palavras de ambição (opportunity, career, grow, challenge, goal, improve) e o produto do bloco: dois minutos de fala contínua, sem um único but. -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 10 -- What Do You Like? What Do You Want?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Paula Morales | Lesson 10 -- What Do You Like? What Do You Want?
lesson.grammar_point: like + gerund (habit/preference) vs want + to-infinitive (direct desire) vs would like + to-infinitive (polite/formal desire)

listenings.text: Hi, Paula. David here. Your career conversation with Greg is on Thursday, and I want to tell you one thing about it, because nobody tells you this. Greg is going to ask you one question: What do you want? And I know exactly what you are going to do, because everybody does it. You are going to say: I'm happy, everything is fine, thank you. And then you are going to go back to your desk and be angry for a year. Here is the thing about English that helps you here. In your language, the word for want sounds like a child asking for chocolate — so you learned to hide it. In English, I want to grow is not rude. It is not arrogant. It is the answer to the question. It is what he asked for. And if you want it softer, you have the same trick you learned last week with can and could: I would like to grow. Same want, with a tie on. Both are correct, Paula. What is not correct is saying nothing. Two more things, and they are small. One: like is about what you already do — I like working with brokers. Say that one first, it is the easy one. Two: after want, the verb needs the to. I want to grow, never I want grow. Last week I told you the verb goes naked after can. This week it wears the to. I know. English does that. See you Thursday.

Good morning, Paula. Sofia here. Thursday, with Greg — one thing, and then I stop. I have watched you for three months. You are the best person in this office with a broker on the phone, and everybody here knows it. Everybody except you. Now, Thursday. He is going to ask what you want, and you are going to want to be modest. Don't. I did that for twelve years. Twelve years of saying I'm fine, everything is good, thank you — and every year somebody else got the opportunity, and I went home and told my husband it was not fair. It was fair, Paula. They asked me what I wanted, and I said nothing. Here is what I want you to understand, and it has nothing to do with English. You think saying what you want is arrogant. It is not. It is information. Greg cannot give you a challenge he does not know you want. He is not a magician — he is a director with a form. Fill in the form. And one last thing, because I know you: your English is not going to be perfect on Thursday, and Greg is not going to care. He is going to hear I want to grow, and he is going to write it down. That is all that happens. See you Thursday.

reading.rtitle: Career Conversations — Zurich Brazil, Internal Page
paras: Once a year, every person at Zurich has a career conversation with a director. It is thirty minutes, and there is only one question: What do you want? Many people arrive and say nothing. They think it is not polite to want things. This page is here to say: it is.

How it works. There are three questions, and you say the answers out loud. First: What do you like about your work today? Not what is easy — what you like. Second: What do you want in twelve months? Say it directly: I want to grow. I want to manage a team. I want to work with international clients. Third: What would you like to learn? This one is the polite one, because you are asking the company for something: I'd like to learn the underwriting side. I'd like to improve my English.

Here are two answers from last year. Ricardo, broker relations, São Paulo: "I like working with brokers. I like it more than anything else in this job, and that is not going to change. But my goal is bigger than my week. I want a challenge — I want to understand the numbers behind the policy, and I'd like to spend one month with the underwriting team." He is with the underwriting team now.

And Sofia, commercial manager: "For twelve years I did not answer this question. I said I was happy, and I went back to my desk. I was not unhappy — I just did not know that wanting something was allowed. Then one year I said it out loud: I want your job. The director laughed, and then he wrote it down. Three years later I had it. Nobody at this company has ever lost an opportunity for saying what they want. People lose opportunities for saying nothing."

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The page explains how to ask your director for more money.
The page is Zurich's career conversation: one question — what do you want? — and it says that saying it out loud is allowed, and that people lose opportunities by saying nothing.
The page says that Ricardo and Sofia are leaving the company.

items: The career conversation has only one question: What do you want?
t
The text says it: "It is thirty minutes, and there is only one question: What do you want?"
The page says it is not polite to say what you want directly.
f
The opposite. It says many people "think it is not polite to want things" — and the page exists "to say: it is". For the twelve-month question it says: "Say it directly."
Ricardo does not like working with brokers.
f

He says the opposite: "I like working with brokers. I like it more than anything else in this job, and that is not going to change." He wants a challenge AND he likes his work — the two live together.
Sofia said what she wanted for the first time after twelve years.
t
"For twelve years I did not answer this question... Then one year I said it out loud: I want your job." Three years later she had it.
Sofia says people lose opportunities when they say what they want.
f
The opposite: "Nobody at this company has ever lost an opportunity for saying what they want. People lose opportunities for saying nothing."

bank.label: What you like / O que você gosta — like + verbo com -ing
items: I like working with brokers.
I like meeting new clients.
I like a challenge.
I don't like working on weekends.
What do you like about your work?

bank.label: What you want / O que você quer — want to · would like to
items: I want to grow in this company.
I'd like to learn the underwriting side.
My goal is to work with international clients.
I'd like to improve my English.
This is a great opportunity for me.

items.situation: Greg starts with the easy one: "What do you like about your work?" Answer in one sentence — and remember what comes after like.
tips: I like working with brokers. Twenty years, and I still like it.

items.situation: Now the real one: what do you want in twelve months? Say it directly — no tie, no apology, no but.
tips: I want to grow in this company.
I want to grow. I want a challenge.

items.situation: Same want, but now you are asking the company to give you something. Put the tie on.
tips: I'd like to learn the underwriting side.
I'd like to work with the international clients.

items.situation: You have one goal for next year. Say it in one sentence, and start with "My goal is".
tips: My goal is to work with international clients.
My goal is to improve my English.

items.situation: Sofia asks if you want the Miami account. You do. Say yes, and say why — use the word opportunity.
tips: Yes. This is a great opportunity for me.
Yes, I want it. This is a great opportunity for me.

items.situation: The director asks what you would like to learn this year. Answer — and do not say your English is bad.
tips: I'd like to improve my English.
I'd like to improve my English, and I'd like to learn the underwriting side.
